comments.text: A negative answer to this problem implies the (finite field) Ajtai-Szemeredi theorem that for $d$ large, every dense subset of $F_2^d \times F_2^d$ contains a square $\{ (a,b), (a,b+r), (a+r,b), (a+r,b+r)\}$.. Indeed, if one could find a dense set $A \subset F_2^d \times F_2^d$ without squares, then one can form the bipartite graph joining $a \in F_2^d $ to $b \in F_2^d$ if $(a,b) \in A$, and color it by $a+b$; as no rectangle in $A$ is a square and we are in characteristic 2, this bipartite graph has all $C_4$s polychromatic and is dense, thus positively answering this problem. By considering other coloring schemes than $a+b$, we see that a negative answer to this problem actually implies quite a lot of Ajtai-Szemeredi type theorems; only one of these has to fail in order to positively answer the question. EDIT: the problem seems vaguely reminiscent of the recently popular topic of skew-corner-free sets. The problem is close to that of asking for $\gg n^2$ points in $\{1,\dots,n\}^3$ that do not contain a pattern of the form $(a,b,c), (a,b',c'), (a',b',c), (a',b,c'')$ (here the third component should be thought of as the "color" of the edge connecting the first two components).

comments.text: Consider the set $S = \{(a,b,a): a,b,\in [N]\}$. Note that is one contains a pattern of the form shown we have that $a = c$ and $a' = c$. This forces $a = a'$ so we don't get a genuine $4$ cycle in the base as $(a,b) = (a',b)$ in this example. This counterexample was given by ChatGPT 5.1 Pro. (This seems to be a ''mistaken'' version of the $(7,4)$ problem presumably?)

comments.text: But this is not a counterexample to the original problem. Terry's reformulation is not literal. The exact (obvious) reformulation reads: does there exist a set $A\subseteq[n]^2$ with $\gg n^2$ elements and a function $f\colon A\to[n]$ such that the graph of $f$ does not contain any of the following $6$ patterns:\[ (a,b), (a,b',c'), (a',b,c''), (a',b',c''') \]with $a\neq a'$, $b\neq b'$ and $c=c'$ or $c=c''$ or $c=c'''$ or $c'=c''$ or $c'=c'''$ or $c''=c'''$. What you wrote doesn't prevent all of them.

comments.text: Thanks for clarifying my post! (This does seem very close to the $(7,4)$ problem; see https://yufeizhao.com/gtacbook/gtacbook.pdf.) Note that the $(7,4)$ problem with loss of generality can be assumed to be on linear hyper graphs; if four hyperedges intersect at an edges this given a $(6,4)$ configuration so an easy pruning argument reduces to a linear hyper graph. Given this linear hypergraph, one may take a random tripartition which preserves a positive density of the edges. It is then a question of how to divide the seven vertices among the three parts. Every part obvious has at least $1$ vertex; however any $(5,1,1)$, $(4,2,1)$ or $(3,3,1)$ would force the hypergraph not to be linear. The $(3,2,2)$ split is then almost exactly the problem mentioned except that we have a ''designated'' part for the $3$ instead of having the freedom where it is placed. Given this connection I expect the problem not to be particularly easy.

comments.text: I believe so? To see this, consider a hypergraph $\mathcal{H}$ with $n$ vertices and $\ge \varepsilon n^2$ hyperedges and suppose that is has no $(7,4)$ configuration. By reducing $\ \varepsilon \to \varepsilon/6$ (say) we may assume $\mathcal{H}$ is linear; this is due to the fact that if $4$ hyperedges all share a common pair of vertices this gives a $(6,4)$ configuration. Once $\mathcal{H}$ is linear we may then apply a random tripartition and get a $\mathcal{H}'$ which is tripartite with parts $A_1,A_2,A_3$ such that $|A_i| = n/3$ and $\mathcal{H}'$ has $\gg \varepsilon n^2$ edges. Define $G$ to be the $2$-graph between $(A_1,A_2)$ and define the coloring of $G$ by identifying by how a given edge extends into third part. A $3$-coloured $C_4$ in $G$ corresponds to a $(7,4)$ configuration in $\mathcal{H}'$ and hence in $\mathcal{H}$ as desired.

comments.text: For anyone who wants to work with explicit examples, I got AlphaEvolve to find the densest n-colored graphs it could on $\{0,\dots,n-1\})$ with all $C_4$'s polychromatic for $n=4,\dots,25$. Here is the list (given as triples $(a,b,c)$ of edges $\{a,b\}$ colored by $c$, together with edge densities). The edge counts are $$ (4,5), (5,7), (6,11), (7,14), (8,17), (9,23), (10,30), (11,34), (12,40), (13,47), (14,54), (15,62), (16,69), (17,77), (18,85), (19,94), (20,103), (21,112), (22,123), (23,133), (24,143), (25,155)$$ (e.g., for $n=25$ the example has 155 edges, for an edge density of $0.5167$. So if the edge density goes to zero, it does so rather slowly (but this is to be expected, given the connection to the Ajtai-Szemeredi theorem). AlphaEvolve gives no guarantee of optimality. Perhaps some SAT-solver experts could work out the optimal edge counts for enough values of $n$ that an OEIS submission could be made.
